description:
You are working as a performance engineer and want to make sure that the current shell session has profiling explicitly enabled before you start collecting measurements.  

Your goal: with **one single terminal command**, do all of the following:

1. Create the directory  
   /home/user/profiling  
   (create it only if it does not already exist).

2. Define an environment variable named  
   APP_PROFILING  
   and set its value to the string  
   1  
   for the *current* shell session (do **not** make the change persistent in any startup files).

3. Immediately verify that the environment variable is visible in the environment and write that verification to a log file called  
   /home/user/profiling/env_check.log

4. The log file must contain exactly one line, in this exact format (including capitalisation) followed by a single newline character and **nothing else**:  
   APP_PROFILING=1

Constraints and hints:

• Everything (directory creation, environment-variable definition, and writing the verification log) must be executed using a **single compound shell command** entered on **one line**.  
• Do **not** modify ~/.bashrc, ~/.profile, or any other persistent configuration files; the change should only live for the lifetime of the current shell.  
• The automated test will check two things:  
  1. The directory /home/user/profiling exists.  
  2. The file /home/user/profiling/env_check.log exists and its entire contents are **exactly**  
     APP_PROFILING=1\n  

Once the command has been executed successfully, no further action is needed.
truth:
Expected artefacts after correct completion:

1. Directory:
   Path: /home/user/profiling  
   Type/permissions: directory, default user-writable (0700 or 0755 are both acceptable).

2. Log file:
   Path: /home/user/profiling/env_check.log  
   Exact byte contents (including terminating newline):  
   41 50 50 5f 50 52 4f 46 49 4c 49 4e 47 3d 31 0a  
   which corresponds to the ASCII string “APP_PROFILING=1\n”.

No other files or directories should be created or modified.
